2001 single by Missy Elliott

This article is about the 2001 song by Missy Elliott. For the 1995 album by Rare Essence, see [Get Your Freak On](/wiki/Get_Your_Freak_On "Get Your Freak On").

"**Get Ur Freak On**" is a song by American rapper [Missy Elliott](/wiki/Missy_Elliott "Missy Elliott"). It was written and produced by Elliott and [Timbaland](/wiki/Timbaland "Timbaland") for her third studio album *[Miss E... So Addictive](/wiki/Miss_E..._So_Addictive "Miss E... So Addictive")* (2001). Based on heavy [bhangra](/wiki/Bhangra_(music) "Bhangra (music)") elements, a popular music and dance form from the region of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab") in [India](/wiki/India "India"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) the song features a six-note base that is a Punjabi melody played on a [tumbi](/wiki/Tumbi "Tumbi") and rhythm and bassline played on [tabla](/wiki/Tabla "Tabla").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Released as the album's first single on March 13, 2001, the track reached number seven on the US [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") chart. Internationally, "Get Ur Freak On" became a top-10 success in the Netherlands and the United Kingdom, where it became her first solo top-10 hit, peaking at number four. A remix of the song featuring [Nelly Furtado](/wiki/Nelly_Furtado "Nelly Furtado") was a dance club hit during this period. On the *Billboard* magazine issue dated February 21, 2015, "Get Ur Freak On" re-entered at number 40, more than a decade after its original chart run. This re-entry was spawned by Missy Elliott's performance at the [Super Bowl XLIX halftime show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIX_halftime_show "Super Bowl XLIX halftime show") that occurred earlier in the month. In 2025, the song was named the greatest of the 21st century by [*Rolling Stone*](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Get_Ur_Freak_On&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

"Get Ur Freak On" was written and produced by Elliott and longtime collaborator [Timbaland](/wiki/Timbaland "Timbaland") for her third studio album, *[Miss E... So Addictive](/wiki/Miss_E..._So_Addictive "Miss E... So Addictive")* (2001). Recorded late in the album’s production, Elliott felt that the project was missing a key element, while Timbaland considered this concern to be overanalyzing.[[5]](#cite_note-AP-5) During this period, Timbaland experimented with a keyboard, creating a series of sounds that eventually formed the basis for the track that would become "Get Ur Freak On."[[5]](#cite_note-AP-5)

## Critical reception and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Get_Ur_Freak_On&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Critical reception and legacy")]

"Get Ur Freak On" is widely considered one of Missy Elliott's best songs. *[Stereogum](/wiki/Stereogum "Stereogum")* and *[Paste](/wiki/Paste_(magazine) "Paste (magazine)")* ranked the song number one and number two, respectively, on their lists of the 10 greatest Missy Elliott Songs.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)
In 2011, the song was listed 14th on *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*'s "Best Songs of the 2000s".[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 2004 and 2010, it was ranked at number 466 on their "[500 Greatest Songs of All Time](/wiki/Rolling_Stone%27s_500_Greatest_Songs_of_All_Time "Rolling Stone's 500 Greatest Songs of All Time")" list.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In the 2021 reranking, it was ranked at number eight.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 2002, "Get Ur Freak On" was named the best single released in the year 2001 by *The Village Voice*'s [Pazz & Jop](/wiki/Pazz_%26_Jop "Pazz & Jop") annual year-end critics' poll. The song also lists at number seven on *[Pitchfork Media](/wiki/Pitchfork_Media "Pitchfork Media")*'s Top 500 Songs of the 2000s and number 16 on [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1")'s "Greatest Songs of Hip-Hop". In 2011, *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* placed it at number 17 on its list "150 Best Tracks of the Past 15 Years".[[11]](#cite_note-11) In April 2014, the song was remixed with [the Black Keys](/wiki/The_Black_Keys "The Black Keys")' song "Keep Me" for the original soundtrack to *[Neighbors](/wiki/Neighbors_(2014_film) "Neighbors (2014 film)")* (2014).[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2002, "Get Ur Freak On" won Elliott [the Grammy Award](/wiki/2002_Grammy_Awards "2002 Grammy Awards") for [Best Rap Solo Performance](/wiki/Best_Rap_Solo_Performance "Best Rap Solo Performance").

## Music video

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Get_Ur_Freak_On&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Music video")]

"Get Ur Freak On" marked the first time that Elliott would team up with director [Dave Meyers](/wiki/Dave_Meyers_(director) "Dave Meyers (director)"), who would become a frequent collaborator on subsequent projects.[[13]](#cite_note-fortune-13) Elliott, a fan of his work, decided to consult Meyers after she had taken him to see *[Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon](/wiki/Crouching_Tiger,_Hidden_Dragon "Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon")* in theatre.[[13]](#cite_note-fortune-13) Nadine "Hi-Hat" Ruffin served as the videos's choreographer.[[14]](#cite_note-bbc-14) In "Get Ur Freak On," Elliott's dancers throw shapes in "some kind of industrial underworld – crouched on concrete blocks, hanging upside down like bats,"[[14]](#cite_note-bbc-14) while Elliott herself act both "queenly and cartoonish: craning her head from her body; swinging from a chandelier; and in one memorably trippy, *[Matrix](/wiki/The_Matrix "The Matrix")*-like effect, spitting long-distance into a male dancer's mouth."[[14]](#cite_note-bbc-14) Rappers [Ludacris](/wiki/Ludacris "Ludacris"), [LL Cool J](/wiki/LL_Cool_J "LL Cool J"), [Timbaland](/wiki/Timbaland "Timbaland"), [Ja Rule](/wiki/Ja_Rule "Ja Rule"), [Busta Rhymes](/wiki/Busta_Rhymes "Busta Rhymes"), [Master P](/wiki/Master_P "Master P"), [Spliff Star](/wiki/Spliff_Star "Spliff Star"), [Lil' Romeo](/wiki/Romeo_Miller "Romeo Miller"), [Eve](/wiki/Eve_(rapper) "Eve (rapper)"), [Nate Dogg](/wiki/Nate_Dogg "Nate Dogg") and singer [Nicole Wray](/wiki/Nicole_Wray "Nicole Wray") make cameo appearances in the video.[[14]](#cite_note-bbc-14)

## Cover versions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Get_Ur_Freak_On&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Cover versions")]

* In 2005, Scottish acoustic rocker [KT Tunstall](/wiki/KT_Tunstall "KT Tunstall") released her version of "Get Ur Freak On" performed for [BBC Radio](/wiki/BBC_Radio "BBC Radio") [Live Lounge](/wiki/Live_Lounge "Live Lounge") on certain versions of the single for "[Suddenly I See](/wiki/Suddenly_I_See "Suddenly I See")".
* [Anda Adam](/wiki/Anda_Adam "Anda Adam")'s single "Nai, Nai" from her debut album *[Confidențial](/wiki/Confiden%C8%9Bial_(album) "Confidențial (album)")* (2005) samples the Elliot's track.
* [Shawn Lee's Ping Pong Orchestra](/wiki/Shawn_Lee_(musician) "Shawn Lee (musician)") released a [surf rock](/wiki/Surf_rock "Surf rock") rendition on the 2007 album "Hits the Hits."

American rock band [Eels](/wiki/Eels_(band) "Eels (band)") also released a version of "Get Ur Freak On" on *[Meet the Eels: Essential Eels Vol. I](/wiki/Meet_the_Eels:_Essential_Eels_Vol._I "Meet the Eels: Essential Eels Vol. I")*, having frequently performed it live during the [Shootenanny!](/wiki/Shootenanny! "Shootenanny!") tour several years previously.

* On the comedy show *[Fonejacker](/wiki/Fonejacker "Fonejacker")*, the track was parodied by [Kayvan Novak](/wiki/Kayvan_Novak "Kayvan Novak") as he tried to get "Sounds of the Universe" record shop manager Jon Burnip, to identify the track by singing the tune with nonsensical lyrics.[[66]](#cite_note-66)
* In [Britney Spears](/wiki/Britney_Spears "Britney Spears")' [Britney: Piece of Me](/wiki/Britney:_Piece_of_Me "Britney: Piece of Me") revamped Vegas residency show, "Get Ur Freak On" was performed as a medley along with other Elliott songs.
* Though not covered, the melody of the song was sampled in the song “ILoveYourAunt” by American rappers A$AP Ferg and $ki Mask the Slump God.
* Japanese experimental music group [Satanicpornocultshop](/wiki/Satanicpornocultshop "Satanicpornocultshop") sampled lyrics from "Get Ur Freak On" in their 2010 songs "[r.i.p.] Tide" and " Pinky."
* During [James Corden](/wiki/James_Corden "James Corden")'s [Carpool Karaoke](/wiki/Carpool_Karaoke "Carpool Karaoke") segment [Michelle Obama](/wiki/Michelle_Obama "Michelle Obama") rapped with Missy Elliott "Get Ur Freak On".[[67]](#cite_note-67)
